original_abstract:
The landscape of higher education research remains a specialized field characterized by porous boundaries. It is often difficult to distinguish between those dedicated exclusively to the subject and scholars from various disciplines who engage with it only intermittently, or between academic researchers and practitioners involved in broader knowledge production. Across Europe, the prominence of the field varies significantly; it is well-established in some nations but remains marginal in others, leading to a diverse range of research themes and institutional foundations. Despite this diversity, a national focus continues to prevail. Furthermore, the dissemination of findings remains uneven across the continent, largely due to the varying degrees of English language integration within national academic cultures.

Since the 1980s, however, international communication and collaboration have expanded substantially. This shift has fostered an increase in comparative analyses and studies addressing cross-national trends, driven both by grassroots academic initiatives and the rising influence of supra-national policies. Today’s researchers view European support for policy-oriented and "targeted" research through a dual lens: while some see it as a set of counter-productive constraints, others recognize it as a vital opportunity to enhance the quality of research and foster a meaningful dialogue that shapes the future of both higher education policy and practice.
humanized_abstract:
Higher education research is still a discipline with leaky borders. It is difficult to say who belongs to the discipline proper and who belongs to other disciplines while occasionally carrying out research in higher education. It is equally difficult to distinguish between academics and practitioners (including the latter’s affiliated knowledge production). While higher education research is a well-established discipline in some countries in Europe, it remains marginal in others and the themes and institutions involved differ a great deal. Overall, however, the focus is still national. There are various reasons for the differential access to higher education research findings in Europe that are largely to do with the level of English used in the various national research cultures.

In the 1980s the extent of international communication and co-operation had increased significantly. In parallel, there has been an expansion of research into comparative studies focusing on issues of national developments as well as on cross-national trends undertaken within a diversity of contexts, i.e. from a variety of bottom-up as well as top-down perspectives. Hence, contemporary research into European funding for policy-oriented and "targeted" research carried out at the Tertiary level is conducted from two quite different perspectives: either as a way of responding to certain undesirable characteristics of European research funding and thereby of thwarting their negative effects, or as a possible, though currently not fully realised, way of promoting research of high quality and thus of ensuring a pertinent and fruitful research-based debate on developments within higher education and its different components.
